title: Generative Data Mining with Longtail-Guided Diffusion
abstract: It is difficult to anticipate the myriad challenges that a predictive model will encounter once deployed. Common practice entails a reactive, cyclical approach: model deployment, data mining, and retraining. We instead develop a proactive longtail discovery process by imagining additional data during training. In particular, we develop general model-based longtail signals, including a differentiable, single forward pass formulation of epistemic uncertainty that does not impact model parameters or predictive performance but can flag rare or hard inputs. We leverage these signals as guidance to generate additional training data from a latent diffusion model in a process we call Longtail Guidance (LTG). Crucially, we can perform LTG without retraining the diffusion model or the predictive model, and we do not need to expose the predictive model to intermediate diffusion states. Data generated by LTG exhibit semantically meaningful variation, yield significant generalization improvements on numerous image classification benchmarks, and can be analyzed by a VLM to proactively discover, textually explain, and address conceptual gaps in a deployed predictive model.
impact: We develop model-based longtail signals, modelconditioned longtail synthetic data generation, and longtail interpretation techniques that have the potential to discover and mitigate production model failure modes before they occur. This has tremendous potential for improving the quality and safety of deployed ML applications. However, they may also have negative impacts if human operators rely too heavily on our approaches and not enough on traditional safeguards. We recommend our methods be used to supplement, not replace, standard deployment practices; most importantly, that regular and satisfactory evaluation on non-synthetic evaluation data be used as a requirement for model deployment.

query_input.scenario: Medical image classifier trained with LTG-generated rare pathology images
query_input.issue: Generated rare-disease images contain subtle anatomical implausibilities, leading to false positives or negatives.
hazard: Diffusion models with long-tail guidance produce synthetic tail-class samples that are out-of-distribution or misrepresent rare concepts.
exposure: Radiologists and patients using AI-assisted diagnostic tools for rare disease screening

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Radiologists' and AI systems' inability to detect subtle synthetic anatomical implausibilities due to scarce real rare-disease reference data.
hipporag_result.impact: False-positive diagnoses trigger unnecessary invasive procedures and anxiety; false-negative diagnoses delay critical treatment, worsening patient outcomes.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Diffusion models generating out-of-distribution rare-disease samples, through AI-assisted screening of rare diseases, trigger radiologists' and AI systems' inability to detect subtle anatomical implausibilities from scarce reference data, leading to false-positive invasive procedures and false-negative treatment delays.

dose_response: Misrepresented rare-disease images cause false positives or negatives, which in AI-assisted screening exposes radiologists and patients to delayed or incorrect treatment.
vulnerability: Clinician over-reliance on AI diagnostic outputs without sufficient rare-disease expertise to detect errors.
Absence of regulatory frameworks for validating synthetic medical data in clinical AI tools.
impact: Delayed or incorrect treatment for patients with rare diseases, worsening health outcomes.
Increased healthcare costs from unnecessary follow-up procedures triggered by false positives.
